content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I was overwhelmed because my work was scattered across too many tools, tabs, and half-finished decisions.
For a long time, I thought my problem was discipline.
I thought I needed better focus. More motivation. A cleaner desk. A stronger routine. Maybe one of those perfect productivity systems that looks beautiful in a YouTube thumbnail and collapses the moment real life enters the room.
But the more honest answer was uncomfortable.
I was not failing because I was lazy. I was failing because my workflow was broken.
Not dramatically broken.
Quietly broken.
The kind of broken where every task begins with five small decisions before the real work even starts.
Where one tab becomes six.
Where research lives in one place, notes in another, screenshots somewhere else, draft ideas in a document I forgot to name, and the final version is buried under three almost-final versions.
That kind of chaos does not feel serious in the beginning.
It feels normal.
Until one day you realize your actual work is not the hard part anymore.
The hard part is finding the mental energy to begin.
The problem was not the work
The work itself was usually clear.
I knew what I wanted to create.
I knew the article I wanted to write, the research I needed to organize, the slide deck I had to prepare, or the small project I kept delaying.
But before I could do the work, I had to collect everything around the work.
Open the notes.
Find the source.
Rewrite the outline.
Check the numbers.
Move the text somewhere cleaner.
Search for the same thing again because I forgot where I saved it.
Then, after all that, I would finally arrive at the task already tired.
That is the part nobody talks about enough.
Most people do not quit because the task is impossible. They quit because the setup is exhausting.
And setup exhaustion is sneaky.
It does not look like procrastination at first.
It looks like “I am just getting ready.”
It looks like “I need one more source.”
It looks like “I should organize this properly before I start.”
It looks productive from the outside.
But inside, you know the truth.
You are circling the work instead of doing it.
Too many tools can make you slower
I used to believe more tools meant more power.
One app for notes.
One app for writing.
One app for research.
One app for images.
One app for slides.
One app for spreadsheets.
One app for saving links.
One app for managing tasks.
Each tool made sense on its own. The problem was the space between them.
That invisible space was where my energy disappeared.
Copying.
Pasting.
Reformatting.
Rechecking.
Summarizing.
Starting again.
Trying to remember what I was doing before I switched windows.
A tool can be useful and still create friction.
That was the lesson I learned slowly.
The issue was not that the tools were bad. The issue was that my work had become a relay race where I was the only runner.
Every time I moved from research to writing, from writing to visuals, from visuals to publishing, I lost speed.
And when you lose speed enough times, you start blaming yourself.
You tell yourself you are inconsistent.
You tell yourself you are not serious enough.
You tell yourself you need more discipline.
But sometimes the honest answer is simpler.
Your system has too many handoffs.
The hidden cost of scattered work
The biggest cost was not time.
Time matters, of course. But the deeper cost was confidence.
When my work was scattered, every project felt heavier than it really was.
A simple article felt like a campaign.
A small presentation felt like a production.
A research task felt like a maze.
And because everything felt heavier, I delayed more often.
Not because I did not care.
Because my brain remembered the friction.
It remembered the last time I opened ten tabs and still did not feel ready.
It remembered the last time I created an outline, lost momentum, came back later, and no longer understood my own notes.
It remembered how many small steps were hiding behind the simple phrase “I’ll just finish this tomorrow.”
The brain avoids what it expects to be messy.
That sentence changed how I looked at productivity.
I stopped asking, “Why am I not motivated?”
I started asking, “Where exactly does this become annoying?”
That question was much more useful.
The friction test I wish I used earlier
Before adding any new tool, I now try to understand the friction first.
Not in a complicated way.
Just honestly.
Here is the small test I use:

• Where does the task slow down before the real work starts?
Is it research, organizing, outlining, writing, formatting, designing, or checking?

• Which step do I keep avoiding even though it is important?
Avoidance usually points to friction, not laziness.

• Am I switching tools because it helps, or because my workflow forces me to?
Tool switching feels harmless until it becomes the workflow.

• What part of this task could be handled in one place instead of five?
This is where the biggest relief usually appears.

• Would a simpler system make me start faster?
Starting faster is underrated. It protects momentum.
That last point matters most.
Because momentum is fragile.
You can destroy it with one unnecessary login, one missing file, one confusing dashboard, or one extra decision at the wrong moment.
The best productivity upgrade is not always doing more. Sometimes it is removing the steps that make you hesitate.
Why all-in-one workspaces started making more sense to me
This is why I started paying more attention to AI workspaces instead of single-purpose tools.
Not because I believe one tool should magically do everything.
I do not.
That is usually marketing nonsense.
But I do think there is real value in reducing the number of places where work gets stuck.
A good AI workspace should not replace judgment.
It should reduce the boring drag around judgment.
It should help you move from idea to draft, from research to structure, from messy notes to something usable, without forcing you to rebuild the context every time.
That is where Genspark AI caught my attention.
Genspark describes itself as an all-in-one AI workspace for slides, docs, images, video, code, and design. Its app listing also frames it around researching, writing, creating, organizing work, building presentations, working with spreadsheets, drafting documents, creating meeting notes, and generating media from prompts.
That positioning matters because it speaks to the real pain.
Not “AI can answer questions.”
We already know that.
The more interesting question is:
Can AI reduce the messy middle between an idea and a finished piece of work?
That is where creators, freelancers, students, marketers, small business owners, and everyday knowledge workers lose so much energy.
Not always in the thinking.
In the stitching.
What Genspark AI seems useful for
I would not describe Genspark AI as a magic productivity fix.
That would be dishonest.
No tool can make a weak idea strong by itself.
No AI workspace can replace taste, editing, experience, judgment, or knowing what your audience actually cares about.
But I can see why a tool like this is attractive.
It is built around the kind of work that usually spreads across multiple tabs: research, writing, documents, slides, visuals, code, and structured outputs.
That is not a small thing.
Because when the workspace is more connected, the task feels less broken.
And when the task feels less broken, starting becomes easier.
A tool does not need to change your life to be useful. It only needs to remove one painful bottleneck.
For me, that is the more realistic way to judge AI tools now.
Not “Will this make me rich?”
Not “Will this make me go viral?”
Not “Will this replace every app I use?”
Those are weak questions.
The better question is:
Does this reduce the part of the work I keep avoiding?
If the answer is yes, it deserves a closer look.
I may earn a small commission if you use my link, at no extra cost to you — but I’m only mentioning it because it fits this exact workflow problem I kept noticing. You can check out Genspark AI here: genspark.ai?via=riyas67
What it does not fix
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is where people get dishonest with AI tools.
So let’s be clear.
Genspark AI will not fix unclear thinking.
It will not make a boring idea automatically interesting.
It will not guarantee traffic.
It will not make Medium distribute your article.
It will not replace editing.
It will not replace taste.
It will not know your audience better than you do.
It will not turn every prompt into something publishable.
Some outputs will need revision.
Some results may be too generic.
Some ideas will still need human judgment.
And sometimes the better choice will still be a simple document, a blank page, or a slower manual process.
That is normal.
AI is not the strategy. AI is support for the strategy.
The strategy is still human.
Know what you are trying to say.
Know who you are trying to help.
Know what problem actually matters.
Know what should be automated and what should stay personal.
That balance is where the real value is.
The real win is less resistance
I think the best tools are not always the ones that feel the most impressive in a demo.
The best tools are often the ones that make the next step feel less annoying.
That sounds small.
It is not.
Less resistance changes behavior.
When research feels easier, you start sooner.
When organizing feels lighter, you keep going.
When drafting feels less intimidating, you make more attempts.
When visuals and documents are closer to the same workflow, you lose less energy switching context.
Consistency is easier when the system stops fighting you.
That is the quiet advantage people underestimate.
Most of us do not need more hype.
We need fewer points where the work falls apart.
We need fewer moments where a simple task becomes a messy chain of extra steps.
We need tools that help us stay inside the work long enough to finish.
Who should actually look at it
Genspark AI makes the most sense for people who create or organize different kinds of work regularly.
Writers who research before drafting.
Creators who need ideas, outlines, visuals, and publishing assets.
Students who need to turn information into structured outputs.
Marketers who move between research, copy, slides, and content planning.
Small business owners who need useful documents but do not want to spend hours starting from scratch.
People who are tired of using six tools just to finish one project.
It may not be necessary for someone who only needs basic chat answers.
It may not be the right fit if your current system is already clean and fast.
It may also not be worth it if you expect the tool to do your thinking for you.
But if your biggest problem is scattered work, too many tabs, and unfinished ideas sitting in different places, then it is at least worth exploring.

The quiet lesson
I used to think productivity was mostly about pushing harder.
Now I think it is more about designing fewer places to get stuck.
That is less glamorous.
But it is more useful.
Because the work will always require effort.
Writing still takes effort.
Thinking still takes effort.
Editing still takes effort.
Making decisions still takes effort.
But the system around the work does not need to punish you before you even begin.
The goal is not to remove effort. The goal is to remove unnecessary friction.
That is the difference.
And once I saw it that way, I stopped blaming myself for every delay.
Sometimes the problem is not your discipline.
Sometimes the problem is the maze you built around the work.
And sometimes the smartest move is not to work harder inside the maze.
It is to make the maze smaller.
